overview.introduction: Bash scripting lets you automate repetitive terminal tasks by writing commands into a reusable script file. Instead of typing 20 commands manually every time, you run one script.
overview.why_it_matters: Every CI/CD pipeline, server setup, and infrastructure task involves shell scripts. Knowing bash scripting is the difference between manual and fully automated DevOps workflows.
overview.industry_usage: Used in CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, Jenkins), server provisioning scripts, cron jobs, Docker entrypoints, Kubernetes init containers, and cloud infrastructure automation.
overview.what_you_will_build: An automated deployment validation script that checks system state, uses variables, reads user input, and applies conditional logic — just like real-world DevOps scripts.
overview.prerequisites: Linux Command Line Basics and File Permissions — you should know how to navigate, create files, and set execute permissions.
learning_outcomes: Create executable bash scripts with the correct shebang line.
Define and use variables to store and reuse values.
Write if/else conditionals to make decisions.
Use for and while loops to automate repetitive tasks.
Read user input and use command-line arguments in scripts.
Use functions to organize and reuse code blocks.

key_concepts.title: The Shebang Line (#!/bin/bash)
key_concepts.definition: The very first line in a script file that tells the system to use Bash to run it.
key_concepts.explanation: Every bash script must start with #!/bin/bash on line 1. Combined with chmod +x, this allows the script to run as a program.

commands.name: #!/bin/bash
commands.output_explanation: Prepares hello.sh to be run by the Bash shell interpreter.

key_concepts.title: Bash Variables
key_concepts.definition: Placeholders used to store and reuse data, like text or numbers.
key_concepts.explanation: Set a variable with NAME=value (no spaces). Read its value later in the script by putting a dollar sign in front: $NAME.

commands.name: Variable Assignment
commands.purpose: Stores a value in a variable.
commands.output_explanation: Stores 'DevOpsUser' inside MY_NAME. Access it using $MY_NAME.

key_concepts.title: Command Substitution $(command)
key_concepts.definition: A way to run a command and save its terminal output inside a variable.
key_concepts.explanation: Surround any command with $(...) to capture what it prints, so you can use that data later in your script.

commands.name: $(command)
commands.output_explanation: Runs the date command and stores the resulting date text inside TODAY.

key_concepts.title: if/else Conditionals
key_concepts.definition: Allows a script to make decisions and run different commands based on rules.
key_concepts.explanation: Use an if statement to check a condition, like checking if a config file exists before trying to read it.

commands.name: if statement
commands.purpose: Branches code execution based on a test.
commands.example: if [ -f hello.sh ]; then echo Found; fi
commands.output_explanation: Prints 'Found' because the file hello.sh exists.

key_concepts.title: for Loops
key_concepts.definition: Repeats a set of commands for each item in a list.
key_concepts.explanation: Use a for loop when you want to run the same command on multiple servers, directories, or files automatically.

commands.name: for loop
commands.purpose: Iterates over a list of values.
commands.syntax: for x in list; do ... done
commands.example: for x in a b c; do echo $x; done
commands.output_explanation: Loops three times, printing a, b, and c on new lines.

interactive_examples.objective: Write and run a simple hello world bash script.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your first bash script — confirm the workflow of creating, permissioning, and running a script.
interactive_examples.command: echo '#!/bin/bash\necho "Hello from DevLab!"' > hello.sh && chmod +x hello.sh && ./hello.sh
interactive_examples.explanation: echo writes the script content, chmod +x makes it executable, ./ runs it from the current directory.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The shebang told the OS to use bash, which executed the echo command inside the script.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Every new CI/CD automation script follows this exact create-permission-run workflow.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Running `hello.sh` without ./ — bash searches PATH directories only, not the current folder.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `bash -x hello.sh` to see each command printed as it runs — great for debugging.

interactive_examples.objective: Use variables to make a script configurable.
interactive_examples.scenario: A deployment script should use variables for the app name and environment so it can be reused.
interactive_examples.explanation: Two variables are set and then referenced inside the echo string. This is the foundation of configurable scripts.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: $APP and $ENV are expanded to their values inside the double-quoted string.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Deployment scripts define APP, VERSION, and ENVIRONMENT at the top, then use them throughout.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Using single quotes: echo 'Deploying $APP' would print literally $APP without expanding it.
interactive_examples.tips: Define all configuration variables at the top of the script, grouped together, for easy editing.

interactive_examples.objective: Check if a file exists before reading it.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your script reads a config file. If the file is missing, the script should print an error and exit gracefully.
interactive_examples.command: if [ -f .env ]; then echo 'Config found'; else echo 'ERROR: .env missing. Exiting.'; fi
interactive_examples.explanation: [-f .env] tests if .env is a regular file. The if/else branches handle both outcomes.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: .env doesn't exist in this example, so the else branch runs with the error message.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Every production script validates required files exist before attempting to use them.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Not checking for file existence — the script crashes with 'No such file or directory' instead of a helpful message.
interactive_examples.tips: Add `exit 1` after the error message to make the script return a non-zero exit code, signaling failure to the calling process.

interactive_examples.objective: Loop over a list of environments to check deployment status.
interactive_examples.scenario: You need to print a status check message for each of your three environments.
interactive_examples.command: for env in development staging production; do echo "[CHECK] Environment: $env"; done
interactive_examples.explanation: The for loop iterates over the three environment names. Each iteration, $env holds the current value and the echo runs.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
[CHECK] Environment: development
[CHECK] Environment: staging
[CHECK] Environment: production
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Three lines are printed — one for each environment in the list.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Loop over server hostnames to SSH into each one and run the same health check command.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting `done` at the end of the loop — bash will wait for more input or throw a syntax error.
interactive_examples.tips: Store the list in a variable: ENVS='dev staging prod' then: for env in $ENVS; do ...

interactive_examples.objective: Capture command output in a variable and use it.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your deployment script needs to log when it runs and who triggered it.
interactive_examples.command: TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S') && USER=$(whoami) && echo "[$TIMESTAMP] Deployment started by $USER"
interactive_examples.explanation: $() captures command output. date formats the current timestamp. whoami gets the current user. Both are stored and used in the log line.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The timestamp and username are dynamically captured and embedded in the log message.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Production deployment scripts log timestamps and the triggering user to an audit trail file.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Using backticks `command` instead of $() — both work but $() is preferred and easier to read.
interactive_examples.tips: Append to a log file: echo "[$TIMESTAMP] Deployment" >> /var/log/deployments.log

lessons.title: 1. Create a Bash Script
lessons.definition: A bash script is a text file containing shell commands, starting with a shebang line.
lessons.explanation: The shebang #!/bin/bash tells the OS which program to use to interpret the script.
lessons.instruction: Create a file named hello.sh and add the shebang line as the first line.

lessons.title: 2. Make Script Executable
lessons.definition: Scripts must have execute permission before they can be run as programs.
lessons.explanation: chmod +x adds the execute bit. Without it, running ./hello.sh gives 'Permission denied'.

lessons.title: 3. Add an Echo Command
lessons.definition: echo prints text to the terminal standard output stream.
lessons.explanation: Add echo 'Hello World' to hello.sh so the script prints output when run.

lessons.title: 4. Define a Variable
lessons.definition: Variables store reusable values. Defined as NAME=value with no spaces around the =.
lessons.explanation: Add NAME=DevLab to hello.sh as a variable and reference it with $NAME.
lessons.instruction: Add a variable MY_NAME=DevOpsUser to hello.sh.

lessons.title: 5. Use a Variable in echo
lessons.definition: Variables are referenced with a $ prefix: $VARIABLE_NAME.
lessons.explanation: Inside double quotes, $VARIABLE is replaced by its value. Use this to print dynamic messages.
lessons.instruction: Add a line that echoes: Hello, $MY_NAME

lessons.title: 6. Capture Command Output
lessons.definition: Command substitution $() runs a command and stores its output in a variable.
lessons.explanation: CURRENT_DATE=$(date) runs date and stores the output in CURRENT_DATE.
lessons.instruction: Add a variable that captures the output of the date command.

lessons.title: 7. Write an if Statement
lessons.definition: if statements execute commands only when a condition is true.
lessons.explanation: Create a script that checks if a file exists using [ -f filename ] inside an if block.
lessons.instruction: Write a script check.sh that echoes 'Found' if hello.sh exists, else 'Missing'.
lessons.hint: Type: echo 'if [ -f hello.sh ]; then echo Found; else echo Missing; fi' > check.sh && chmod +x check.sh && ./check.sh
lessons.solution: echo 'if [ -f hello.sh ]; then echo Found; else echo Missing; fi' > check.sh && chmod +x check.sh && ./check.sh

lessons.title: 8. Write a for Loop
lessons.definition: for loops iterate over a list of values and run commands for each one.
lessons.explanation: Use `for item in list; do commands; done` to process multiple items.
lessons.instruction: Write a one-liner for loop that echoes 'Item: X' for X in a b c.
lessons.example: for x in 1 2 3; do echo $x; done
lessons.expected:
Item: a
Item: b
Item: c
lessons.hint: Type: for item in a b c; do echo "Item: $item"; done
lessons.solution: for item in a b c; do echo "Item: $item"; done

exercises.title: Hello World Script
exercises.problem: Create a script `greet.sh` that prints 'Welcome to DevLab!' when executed.
exercises.hint: Start with #!/bin/bash, add an echo, then chmod +x, then ./greet.sh.
exercises.objective: Master the basic script creation workflow.

exercises.title: Info Script
exercises.problem: Create `info.sh` that prints: your username (whoami), current directory (pwd), and current date — each on a separate line.
exercises.hint: Use three echo statements with command substitution: echo $(whoami), echo $(pwd), echo $(date).
exercises.expected_result: Three lines showing username, directory, and date.
exercises.objective: Practice using command substitution in scripts.

exercises.title: Variable Config Script
exercises.problem: Create a script with variables for APP_NAME, VERSION, and ENVIRONMENT, then print: 'Deploying APP_NAME v VERSION to ENVIRONMENT'.
exercises.hint: Define three variables, then echo with all three referenced: echo "Deploying $APP_NAME v$VERSION to $ENVIRONMENT"
exercises.objective: Understand how variables make scripts configurable.

exercises.title: File Existence Validator
exercises.problem: Write a script `validate.sh` that checks if `.env` exists. If it does, print 'Config OK'. If not, print 'ERROR: .env not found' and exit with code 1.
exercises.hint: Use `if [ -f .env ]; then echo 'Config OK'; else echo 'ERROR: .env not found'; exit 1; fi`
exercises.expected_result: Prints appropriate message based on whether .env exists.
exercises.objective: Practice defensive scripting with file existence checks.

exercises.title: Multi-Environment Deployer
exercises.problem: Write a script that loops over environments: dev, staging, prod — and prints 'Deploying to: ENV' for each.
exercises.hint: Use a for loop: for env in dev staging prod; do echo "Deploying to: $env"; done
exercises.expected_result: Three lines: Deploying to: dev, Deploying to: staging, Deploying to: prod
exercises.objective: Apply for loops to real-world multi-environment deployment scenarios.

exercises.title: Retry Logic with While
exercises.problem: Write a script that simulates a connection retry — loop up to 3 times, printing 'Attempt N of 3', then print 'Giving up' after the loop.
exercises.hint: Use `ATTEMPT=1; while [ $ATTEMPT -le 3 ]; do echo "Attempt $ATTEMPT of 3"; ATTEMPT=$((ATTEMPT+1)); done; echo 'Giving up'`
exercises.expected_result: Three attempt lines followed by 'Giving up'.
exercises.objective: Implement retry logic — a fundamental pattern in DevOps automation.

exercises.title: Argument Validator
exercises.problem: Create a script `deploy.sh` that requires exactly one argument (environment name). If none provided, print usage and exit 1. If provided, print 'Deploying to: ARG'.
exercises.hint: Use `if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then echo 'Usage: deploy.sh <environment>'; exit 1; fi` then `echo "Deploying to: $1"`
exercises.expected_result: ./deploy.sh production prints 'Deploying to: production'. ./deploy.sh alone prints usage and exits.
exercises.objective: Learn to validate script arguments — essential for production scripts.

exercises.title: Log File Processor
exercises.problem: Create a script that loops over all .log files in the current directory and prints 'Processing: FILENAME (SIZE bytes)' for each. Use stat or ls to get size.
exercises.hint: Use `for f in *.log; do SIZE=$(wc -c < $f); echo "Processing: $f ($SIZE bytes)"; done`
exercises.expected_result: One line per log file with its name and size.
exercises.objective: Practice glob patterns and command substitution inside loops.

exercises.title: Deployment Checklist Script
exercises.problem: Write a script that checks: (1) .env exists, (2) deploy.sh exists and is executable, (3) a 'logs' directory exists. Print OK or FAIL for each check.
exercises.hint: Use three separate if blocks. Check -f for files, -x for executable, -d for directory.
exercises.expected_result: Three status lines showing OK or FAIL for each item.
exercises.objective: Combine multiple conditional checks into a real-world pre-deployment validation script.

exercises.title: System Health Report
exercises.problem: Write a script `health.sh` that captures: current user, hostname, disk usage (df -h), memory usage (free -h), and writes them to a report file `health_report.txt`.
exercises.hint: Use echo with >> to append each section. Use $(hostname), $(whoami), $(df -h), $(free -h) with command substitution.
exercises.expected_result: health_report.txt contains all system info in readable format.
exercises.objective: Build a real-world system health monitoring script from scratch.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the shebang line `#!/bin/bash`?
options: It's a comment that documents the author
It specifies which interpreter runs the script
It defines the first variable in the script
quiz.answer: It specifies which interpreter runs the script
quiz.explanation: The shebang (#!) followed by the path tells the OS which program to use to execute the file. Without it, the OS guesses the interpreter.

incorrect_explanations.It's a comment that documents the author: The # makes it look like a comment but the #! combination has special OS meaning.
incorrect_explanations.It makes the script executable: chmod +x makes it executable. The shebang specifies the interpreter.
incorrect_explanations.It defines the first variable in the scr: Variables are defined as NAME=value, not with #!.

quiz.question: What is wrong with this variable assignment: `MY_VAR = hello`?
options: There should be no spaces around the = sign
quiz.answer: There should be no spaces around the = sign
quiz.explanation: Bash is strict: `MY_VAR=hello` is correct. `MY_VAR = hello` fails because bash tries to run MY_VAR as a command with = and hello as arguments.

incorrect_explanations.MY_VAR should be lowercase: Uppercase is convention for environment variables — it's not required.
incorrect_explanations.hello should be in quotes: Quotes are needed for values with spaces. 'hello' (no spaces) works without quotes.
incorrect_explanations.Nothing is wrong: The spaces around = will cause a bash syntax error.

quiz.question: How do you capture the output of `whoami` into a variable called USER_NAME?
quiz.explanation: $() is command substitution — it runs the command inside and captures its output.

incorrect_explanations.USER_NAME=whoami: This assigns the literal string 'whoami' — not the output of running it.
incorrect_explanations.USER_NAME=`${whoami}`: Backticks work but ${whoami} is wrong — should be `whoami` or $(whoami).
incorrect_explanations.$USER_NAME=whoami: $USER_NAME on the left side tries to expand the variable, not assign to it.

quiz.question: Which test checks if a file exists and is a regular file?
quiz.explanation: -f tests for a regular file specifically. -d tests for a directory. -e tests for any path (file or directory).

incorrect_explanations.[ -d filename ]: -d tests for a DIRECTORY, not a regular file.
incorrect_explanations.[ -e filename ]: -e tests if the path exists at all — could be a file or directory.
incorrect_explanations.[ -r filename ]: -r tests if the file is readable — it would fail if the file doesn't exist.

quiz.question: What is the output of: `for x in 1 2 3; do echo $x; done`?
quiz.explanation: The loop runs echo three times, once for each value. echo prints a newline after each call.

incorrect_explanations.1 2 3 (on one line): echo adds a newline after each print, so each number is on its own line.
incorrect_explanations.x x x: $x is expanded to the current value, not the literal letter x.
incorrect_explanations.An error: This is valid bash syntax and runs without errors.

quiz.question: How do you run a bash script called deploy.sh from the current directory?
quiz.explanation: ./deploy.sh runs using the shebang. bash deploy.sh explicitly uses bash. Just deploy.sh fails because the current directory isn't in PATH.

incorrect_explanations.deploy.sh: Without ./, bash searches PATH directories only. Current directory is not in PATH by default.
incorrect_explanations.run deploy.sh: run is not a valid bash command.
incorrect_explanations.execute deploy.sh: execute is not a valid bash command.

quiz.question: What does `set -e` at the top of a bash script do?
options: Makes the script exit immediately if any command fails
quiz.answer: Makes the script exit immediately if any command fails
quiz.explanation: set -e (errexit) causes bash to exit immediately when any command returns a non-zero exit code. Without it, scripts continue even after failures.

incorrect_explanations.Enables verbose debug output: set -v enables verbose mode. set -x enables tracing.
incorrect_explanations.Sets environment variables: set -e has nothing to do with environment variables.
incorrect_explanations.Enables script tracing: set -x enables tracing (prints each command before running it).

quiz.question: Scenario: Your script fails silently — it runs to completion but doesn't do what you expect. Which flag helps debug it?
quiz.explanation: bash -x (xtrace) prints each command and its expanded arguments as they execute, making it easy to see exactly what the script is doing step by step.

incorrect_explanations.bash -n script.sh: -n does syntax checking only — it doesn't run the script at all.
incorrect_explanations.bash -e script.sh: -e exits on error — doesn't show what's happening.
incorrect_explanations.bash -v script.sh: -v echoes script lines as read (before variable expansion) — less useful than -x.

quiz.question: What does `$?` represent in a bash script?
options: The exit code of the last executed command
quiz.answer: The exit code of the last executed command
quiz.explanation: $? holds the exit code of the most recently executed command. 0 = success, non-zero = failure.

quiz.question: What is the difference between single quotes and double quotes in bash?
options: No difference — both work the same way
Double quotes expand variables; single quotes treat everything as literal
Single quotes expand variables; double quotes don't
Double quotes are for numbers; single quotes are for strings
quiz.answer: Double quotes expand variables; single quotes treat everything as literal
quiz.explanation: echo "Hello $USER" prints 'Hello student' (expands $USER). echo 'Hello $USER' prints literally 'Hello $USER'.

incorrect_explanations.No difference — both work the same way: They behave differently for variable expansion and special characters.
incorrect_explanations.Single quotes expand variables; double q: This is the reverse of how bash actually works.
incorrect_explanations.Double quotes are for numbers; single qu: Both handle strings. The difference is variable/escape expansion.

quiz.question: How do you access the first argument passed to a script?
quiz.explanation: $1 is the first positional argument. $0 is the script name. $2 is the second argument, and so on.

incorrect_explanations.$0: $0 is the name of the script itself, not the first argument.
incorrect_explanations.$ARG: This is not a special variable — it would be undefined unless you set it.
incorrect_explanations.$FIRST: This is not a special variable — you would have to define it manually.

quiz.question: Scenario: You need to loop through all .txt files in a directory and echo their names. Which command is correct?
options: for f in *.txt; do echo $f; done
for f in .txt; do echo; done
quiz.answer: for f in *.txt; do echo $f; done
quiz.explanation: *.txt is a glob pattern matching all .txt files. The for loop assigns each filename to f, and echo $f prints it.

incorrect_explanations.loop *.txt echo: loop is not a valid bash command.
incorrect_explanations.for f in .txt; do echo; done: .txt without * only matches a file literally named '.txt'. Also echo needs $f.
incorrect_explanations.while *.txt; do echo $f; done: while expects a command/condition, not a glob pattern.

resources.summary: Bash scripting automates multi-step tasks. Scripts start with #!/bin/bash, use variables to avoid hardcoding, if/else for decisions, for/while for repetition, and $() to capture command output. These fundamentals power every DevOps pipeline.

resources.cheat_sheet:
# Bash Scripting Cheat Sheet

## Script Structure
#!/bin/bash          # Shebang — must be line 1
set -e               # Exit on any error
set -x               # Debug: print each command

## Variables
NAME="value"         # Assign (no spaces around =)
echo $NAME           # Read variable
echo ${NAME}         # Safer with braces
DATE=$(date)         # Command substitution

## Special Variables
$0    Script name
$1    First argument
$#    Number of arguments
$?    Last command exit code
$$    Current process ID

## Conditionals
if [ -f file ]; then ... fi      # File exists
if [ -d dir ]; then ... fi       # Directory exists
if [ $x -eq 5 ]; then ... fi     # Numbers equal
if [ "$s" = "hi" ]; then ... fi  # Strings equal

## Loops
for x in a b c; do echo $x; done
for f in *.sh; do echo $f; done
while [ $i -lt 5 ]; do ... done

## Quotes
"double"  — variables expand: $VAR → value
'single'  — literal: $VAR stays as $VAR

## Functions
my_func() { echo "Hello $1"; }
my_func World    # Call: Hello World

commands_table.name: #!/bin/bash
commands_table.description: Declare bash as the script interpreter.

commands_table.name: $(cmd)
commands_table.description: Capture command output into a variable.

commands_table.name: for loop
commands_table.syntax: for x in list; do ... done

revision_notes: #!/bin/bash must be the very first line — no blank lines before it.
No spaces around = in variable assignment: NAME=value not NAME = value.
Use double quotes for strings with variables. Use single quotes for literals.
[ condition ] requires spaces inside the brackets.
Every for/while loop needs do ... done. Every if needs fi.
best_practices: Add set -e as the second line of every production script.
Validate all required arguments and files exist before proceeding.
Quote all variable expansions: "$VAR" not $VAR to handle spaces.
Use bash -x script.sh for debugging — it prints every command as it runs.
beginner_mistakes: Forgetting chmod +x before running a script.
Adding spaces around = in variable assignment.
Missing do or done in loops — causes syntax error.
Using single quotes when you want variable expansion.

interview_questions.question: What does set -e do in a bash script?
interview_questions.answer: set -e (errexit) makes the script exit immediately if any command returns a non-zero exit code. Without it, scripts continue running even after failures, potentially causing worse downstream issues.

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between $() and backticks for command substitution?
interview_questions.answer: Both capture command output. $() is preferred: it's more readable, nests easily ($(cmd $(inner))), and is standard in modern bash. Backticks are legacy syntax.

books.title: Learning the bash Shell — Cameron Newham
books.description: O'Reilly's comprehensive guide to bash scripting for beginners and intermediate users.

books.title: The Linux Command Line — William Shotts
books.description: Part 4 covers shell scripting, functions, and automation in depth.
